summary: In this plenary lecture, Dr. Virginia M.-Y. Lee discusses the transmission and strain hypotheses of neurodegenerative diseases. She details how misfolded proteins (tau, alpha-synuclein, TDP-43) spread cell-to-cell and how different conformational strains dictate distinct clinical phenotypes. She emphasizes the limitations of transgenic and synthetic fibril models, advocating instead for the use of patient-derived brain extracts and in vitro amplification techniques to accurately model and study these diseases.

principles.statement: Avoid transgenic overexpression to model sporadic neurodegenerative diseases.
principles.rationale: Most human cases of diseases like Parkinson's are sporadic and do not involve gene duplication or overexpression. Injecting preformed fibrils or brain extracts into wild-type mice provides a more accurate model of disease progression.
principles.source_quote: how do you model sporic disease that that do not have overexpression... we decided that we do not want to use trenic animals because of the fact that you know when you overexpress disease protein... that's not found in in humans

principles.statement: Patient-derived pathological aggregates are fundamentally different and more potent than synthetic preformed fibrils.
principles.rationale: Recombinant preformed fibrils (PFFs) generated in a test tube often fail to induce the full spectrum of human pathology (e.g., causing mostly neuritic rather than somatic inclusions). Human brain extracts induce authentic, strain-specific cellular responses.
principles.source_quote: the material that we isolated from human brain whether or not from Ed brain or PDD brain they're much more potent than the material that we generate in a test tube

principles.statement: Clinical diversity in neurodegenerative diseases is driven by distinct conformational strains of the same misfolded protein.
principles.rationale: Different diseases characterized by the same protein (e.g., AD, CBD, and PSP for tau) exhibit different cell-type specificities, brain region selectivities, and glial involvement because the underlying protein adopts different structural conformations.
principles.source_quote: if now to in the different tauopathies exist with different confirmation... are these different pathology have different cell type specificity and different brain region selectivity

frameworks.name: In Vitro Amplification of Pathological Strains
frameworks.when_to_apply: When patient-derived brain extracts (like Lewy bodies) are scarce, but large quantities of biologically active, strain-specific material are needed for research or therapeutic development.
steps: Extract insoluble pathological seeds (e.g., Lewy bodies) from human diseased brains.
Incubate the human seeds with recombinant monomer (e.g., alpha-synuclein) to amplify the conformation, generating 'P1' material.
Take a small percentage (e.g., 5%) of the P1 amplified material and incubate it again with recombinant monomer to generate 'P2' material.
Verify that the amplified material maintains the unique pathological phenotypes (e.g., somatic inclusions, specific cleavage patterns) of the original diseased brain material.
frameworks.source_quote: we can take the seeds what we call the initial material from ad brain and PDD brain and that if we can amplify the confirmation from these seats with offic nulean recombinant offic nucle monomer... generating Aggregates to maintain the unique pathological phenotypes of the original material

mental_models.name: The Transmission Cascade
mental_models.description: Viewing the progression of neurodegenerative disease as a multi-step cell-to-cell transmission process. This model breaks down disease spread into distinct, targetable steps: release from the donor cell, uptake by the recipient cell, recruitment/seeding of endogenous proteins, and extracellular degradation.
mental_models.example: When designing therapeutics, you can use this model to identify intervention points: 'you can block the release of the moted protein... or you can block the uptake... or you can prevent seeding and and also finally you can promote decreation'.

mental_models.name: Brain Trash (Cross-Seeding Environment)
mental_models.description: The concept that the accumulation of one type of misfolded protein, combined with aging and cellular stress, creates a toxic environment that promotes the misfolding and aggregation of entirely different proteins.
mental_models.example: Mice with high levels of beta-amyloid develop significantly more Lewy bodies when injected with alpha-synuclein compared to wild-type mice, demonstrating how one pathology promotes another.

heuristics: When injecting fibrils into animal models, match the species of the fibril to the host (e.g., use mouse alpha-synuclein for mice) to avoid prion-like species barriers.
When extracting Lewy bodies from brain tissue, you must first remove the myelin, otherwise it will interfere with the extraction.

signature_quotes.text: each of the misfa disease protein behave very very differently even when you try to generate an animal model
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing the distinct behaviors of Tau, Alpha-synuclein, and TDP-43 in experimental models.

signature_quotes.text: the material that we isolated from human brain... they're much more potent than the material that we generate in a test tube
signature_quotes.context: Comparing the efficacy of patient-derived Lewy body extracts versus recombinant preformed fibrils.

signature_quotes.text: if the brain have what we call Brain trash... when you have already a lot of brain trash and the cells are not doing well and um and also age is another factor that all of this come together to promote the development of other type of pathology
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the cross-seeding concept where one pathology (like amyloid) promotes another (like synuclein).

anti_patterns.description: Relying solely on transgenic overexpression models to study sporadic neurodegenerative diseases.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It forces protein aggregation through artificial abundance rather than natural seeding, failing to accurately mimic the sporadic nature of diseases where gene duplication is rare.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming synthetic or recombinant preformed fibrils (PFFs) perfectly mimic human disease pathology.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Recombinant PFFs often fail to induce the full spectrum of human pathology, such as somatic inclusions or glial pathology, eliciting a cellular response distinct from actual human disease strains.

anti_patterns.description: Using heparin to aggregate tau for cell-to-cell transmission models.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Heparin inhibits the internalization of misfolded proteins into cells, preventing the very cell-to-cell transmission the model is attempting to study.
